title: Quiz: Quantum vs Classical Computing
description: Test your understanding of the differences between quantum and classical computing

questions.question: What is the key difference between a classical bit and a qubit?

options.text: Qubits can exist in superposition of 0 and 1 simultaneously
Qubits store more data than bits
Qubits are more reliable than bits

questions.explanation: The fundamental difference is that qubits can exist in a superposition of |0⟩ and |1⟩ simultaneously, written as α|0⟩ + β|1⟩. Classical bits must be in exactly one state at a time.

questions.question: What is quantum parallelism?

options.text: The ability to represent 2ⁿ states with n qubits in superposition
Running quantum algorithms in parallel threads
The speed advantage of quantum over classical

questions.explanation: Quantum parallelism refers to the fact that n qubits in superposition can represent all 2ⁿ possible states simultaneously, and a single operation acts on all states at once.

questions.question: Quantum computers are faster than classical computers for all types of problems.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum computers only provide speedup for specific problem types (like factoring, search, quantum simulation). For most everyday tasks, classical computers are faster, more reliable, and more practical.

questions.question: What enables quantum algorithms to achieve speedup?
questions.explanation: Quantum speedup comes from interference - the ability of quantum amplitudes to add constructively (amplifying correct answers) or destructively (canceling wrong answers). This is not possible with classical probabilities.

questions.question: Which problems are quantum computers expected to excel at? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Quantum computers excel at integer factoring (Shor's algorithm), quantum simulation, and database search (Grover's algorithm). Web browsing is better handled by classical computers.

questions.question: What type of speedup does Grover's search algorithm provide?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides a quadratic speedup: it searches an unsorted database of N items in O(√N) time instead of O(N) classically. For N = 1 million, this means ~1000 queries instead of ~1 million.

questions.question: Why will quantum computers NOT replace classical computers entirely?

options.text: Classical computers are better for most everyday tasks

questions.explanation: All factors contribute: quantum computers are expensive, require extreme conditions (millikelvin temperatures), have high error rates, and are only advantageous for specific problem types. Classical computers remain superior for general-purpose computing.

questions.question: The future of computing is likely to be hybrid, with classical and quantum computers working together.
questions.explanation: True! Hybrid computing is the expected paradigm: classical computers handle data loading, preprocessing, circuit compilation, and postprocessing, while quantum computers execute the quantum parts of algorithms that benefit from quantum speedup.
